messages.say:
Thought for 10s


Registry files



What this category is


Single-source-of-truth (SSOT) datasets that declare system facts (files, columns, policies, derivations, mappings) and are read by gates/CLIs to enforce determinism.


File attributes that qualify


Location signals
REGISTRY/ (or the dir-id prefixed equivalent, e.g., ..._REGISTRY/).
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE
REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT

Name/pattern signals
Contains registry / governance_registry in filename.
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE

Content signals
Has a column_headers dictionary (explicit schema of record fields).
01999000042260124503_governance…

Has a top-level list like files: [...] with per-file records (e.g., file_id, relative_path, layer, artifact_kind, dependency lists).
01999000042260124503_governance…

Tracks path normalization fields like relative_path, directory_path, absolute_path when directory renames happen.
REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT

Registry-metadata signals (strong indicators)
layer often GOVERNANCE (but not always; registry can contain mixed layers).
01999000042260124503_governance…

governance_domain, canonicality, bundle_key, geu_role present/used.
01999000042260124503_governance…



ID files (ID management system)



What this category is


Anything that allocates, validates, tracks, or enforces identity (file_id/dir_id/doc_id/enhanced_id), including counters, canonical regex patterns, and pre-commit gates.


File attributes that qualify


Name/pattern signals
*ID_CANONICALITY*, *COUNTER_STORE*, *id_allocator*, *_id_patterns*
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE
01260207201000000134_FINAL_EXEC…

Content signals
Defines canonical regex patterns like ^\d{20}$, ^P_\d{20}$ (pattern library).
01260207201000000134_FINAL_EXEC…

Implements allocation facade / allocator entrypoint functions (e.g., allocate_file_id, allocate_dir_id).
01260207201000000134_FINAL_EXEC…

Contains a counter store keyed by scope/namespace/type (monotonic counters).
01260207201000000116_COUNTER_ST…

Enforces “no direct imports of internal allocators / no bespoke ID regex” via a gate script.
01260207201000000134_FINAL_EXEC…

Bulk renaming / recursive ID assignment scripts (walk tree, rename files, allocate IDs).
P_01260207201000000198_add_ids_…

Location signals
Often in govreg_core/, gates/, contracts/ (identity laws), and may have dedicated ID/ areas.
01260207201000000134_FINAL_EXEC…
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE

Operational signals
Produces/consumes ID inventories and blocks deprecated/internal ID modules.
01260207201000000134_FINAL_EXEC…



Multi-CLI conversion planning files



What this category is


Plans + specs that define how multiple command-line tools interoperate (message passing, artifacts as messages, conversion/bridge steps, phase sequencing, validations) — i.e., “how CLIs work together,” not the registry itself.


File attributes that qualify


Location signals
LP_LONG_PLAN/, sections/, “PHASE_*” planning directories.
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE

Name/pattern signals
*PHASE_*, sec_*.json, *phase_*, *PLAN*, *BRIDGE*, *CONVERTER*
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE
01999000042260124503_governance…

Content signals
Declares step DAGs, phases, and required artifacts/validations (plan-as-data).
01999000042260124503_governance…

Uses explicit schemas/configs for planning validators and bridge/converter modules (example paths show “CORE-CONVERTERS … BRIDGE … validator.py” style artifacts in planning folders).
01999000042260124503_governance…

Produces evidence/metrics outputs per phase (often under .state/evidence/... and .state/metrics/...).
01999000042260124503_governance…

Distinguishing rule
If it describes how multiple CLIs coordinate via artifacts/evidence and phase contracts (rather than directly implementing the runtime tool), it belongs here.



Template operations files



What this category is


Anything that creates/edits/applies templates or enforces template-driven transformations (patch application, template expansion, deterministic rewrite strategy selection, template validation).


File attributes that qualify


Location signals
templates/ (or equivalent), PATCHES/ for patch artifacts, scripts/ tooling that applies template mutations.
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE
REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT

Name/pattern signals
*_template.*, *.template.*, *.patch.json / patch.rfc6902.json
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE
01999000042260124503_governance…

Content signals
RFC-6902 patch operations (add/replace/remove with JSON Pointer paths).
01999000042260124503_governance…

“Apply patch atomically” / deterministic file rewrite tooling (script-level operations).
01999000042260124503_governance…

Template placeholders / variable markers (e.g., {{VAR}}).
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE

Template-driven registry sync/update scripts (reads registry + filesystem, updates paths/records) — these are “template ops” if their job is to keep derived/template-driven state aligned.
P_01260207201000000197_sync_reg…
REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT

Distinguishing rule
If its primary job is “take an input spec/template + produce or mutate outputs deterministically (often via patches),” it belongs here.



Practical “fast classifier” for your four modules


Registry files: SSOT datasets with column_headers / files[] records and registry path fields.
01999000042260124503_governance…
REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT

ID files: allocators, canonical patterns, ID gates, counter stores, ID rename scripts.
01260207201000000134_FINAL_EXEC…
01260207201000000116_COUNTER_ST…
P_01260207201000000198_add_ids_…

Multi-CLI conversion planning: phase/section planning artifacts + bridge/converter specs coordinating multiple tools.
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE
01999000042260124503_governance…

Template operations: templates + patch artifacts + patch/transform runners that mutate templates/specs deterministically.
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE
01999000042260124503_governance…

Establish the target “section folders” and their dir_ids
Identify the canonical folders (the ones you will eventually move files into):

REGISTRY_FILES/

ID_FILES/

MULTI_CLI_CONVERSION_PLANNING/

TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS/


Ensure each folder already exists in the repo and already has a dir_id assigned (or run the dir-id allocator on just those folders).

Create a small SSOT mapping file (even if temporary) that pins:
section_key → section_folder_path → section_dir_id
Lock down the classification rules (deterministic)

Define a rule set that classifies a file into exactly one of the four sections using ordered precedence.

For each section, define “match signals” (path patterns, filename patterns, content markers if needed).

Output format should be machine-usable (JSON/YAML), e.g.:
rules[]: {section_key, priority, match: {path_globs[], name_regex[], content_regex[]}, exclude...}
Choose the registry update target (what exactly gets patched)

Confirm which registry field is being updated:

module_id in the registry “files” records (not entries).

Confirm what value it must take:
the dir_id of the section folder (not the file’s own dir_id).
Build the candidate set (inventory)

Scan registry files[] records and produce a deterministic report:

file_id

relative_path

current_module_id

detected_section_key

target_section_dir_id

classification_rule_id (which rule matched)

confidence only if you allow ambiguity; otherwise “match/no match”

Hard requirement: any file that matches 0 or >1 sections must be emitted as an exception and block automatic patching.
Decide on “source of truth” for classification input
Pick one (don’t mix without explicit precedence):


Registry-first: classify using registry metadata (layer/artifact_kind/governance_domain) + path/name.

Filesystem-first: classify using paths/names from disk, then reconcile with registry.
Given your goal is updating the registry, registry-first with path/name fallback is usually the most fail-closed.
Generate a patch-only mutation set (RFC-6902)


For every file record where:

detected_section_key is exactly one section, and

current_module_id != target_section_dir_id
generate patch ops:

replace /files/<index>/module_id with <target_section_dir_id>

Also generate a separate “no-op” list (already correct) for audit.
Apply patches with guardrails


Pre-conditions:

Registry schema validates.

Registry hash recorded (before).

Apply patch file atomically.

Post-conditions:
Registry schema validates.

No duplicate file_ids introduced.

No records lost (count invariant).

Registry hash recorded (after).
Produce evidence artifacts (so this is gateable)
Minimum evidence bundle:

classification_rules.(json|yaml) (the rules SSOT used)

classification_report.json (all candidates + exceptions)

registry_patch.rfc6902.json

before_after_hashes.json

apply_log.jsonl (what changed)
Add/extend a gate so drift can’t come back


A gate that re-runs classification and asserts:

any file matching a section must have module_id == section_dir_id

no file matches multiple sections

no “unclassified” files inside those known module areas (optional)
Only after this: move files later

When you eventually move files, you’ll update:

relative_path / directory linkage fields

dir-id registry/path update artifacts

The point of doing module_id now is you can move later without losing “module ownership” tracking.


If you want this to stay fully deterministic: treat steps 2, 4, 6, 8 as mandatory artifacts, and fail closed on ambiguity (multiple matches / no match) instead of guessing.

Rule evaluation contract (deterministic)


Evaluate in priority order (1 = highest).

A file is classified by the first rule that matches.

If a file matches multiple rules at the same priority, it is AMBIGUOUS and must not be auto-patched.

If a file matches none, it is UNCLASSIFIED (no auto-patch).

Exclusions are applied before matches (fail-closed).

Path is the primary signal; name is secondary; content/metadata are tertiary.



Concrete rule set (YAML)


version: "1.0.0"
classifier_id: "module_section_classifier_v1"
default_outcome: "UNCLASSIFIED"
outcomes:
  - REGISTRY_FILES
  - ID_FILES
  - MULTI_CLI_CONVERSION_PLANNING
  - TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS
  - AMBIGUOUS
  - UNCLASSIFIED

# Normalization requirements for matching
normalization:
  path_separator: "/"
  case_sensitive: false
  trim_prefixes:
    - "./"
    - ".\\"

# Global excludes: if any match, file is ignored for classification + patching.
global_excludes:
  path_globs:
    - "**/.git/**"
    - "**/.state/**"
    - "**/.venv/**"
    - "**/venv/**"
    - "**/__pycache__/**"
    - "**/*.pyc"
    - "**/*.pyo"
    - "**/*.log"
    - "**/*.tmp"
    - "**/*.bak"
    - "**/*.zip"
    - "**/*.7z"

rules:

  # ------------------------------
  # 1) REGISTRY_FILES (highest priority)
  # ------------------------------
  - rule_id: "REG-001"
    priority: 1
    section: "REGISTRY_FILES"
    match_any:
      path_globs:
        - "**/REGISTRY/**"
        - "**/*REGISTRY*/**"
      name_regex:
        - "(?i)^.*registry.*\\.(json|yaml|yml|jsonl|csv|md)$"
      content_regex:
        # Registry "shape" markers (SSOT dataset)
        - "\"column_headers\"\\s*:"
        - "\"files\"\\s*:\\s*\\["
    exclude_any:
      # Avoid classifying ID stores as "registry" if they are clearly ID stores (handled by ID rules)
      name_regex:
        - "(?i)counter_store"
        - "(?i)id_canonicality"
        - "(?i)id_patterns"

  # ------------------------------
  # 2) ID_FILES
  # ------------------------------
  - rule_id: "ID-001"
    priority: 2
    section: "ID_FILES"
    match_any:
      path_globs:
        - "**/ID/**"
        - "**/*ID*/**"
        - "**/identity/**"
        - "**/allocat*/**"
        - "**/validators/**id*/**"
      name_regex:
        - "(?i)^P_\\d{20}_.*\\.py$"
        - "(?i)^.*(id_allocator|allocate_.*id|id_assign|add_ids|sync_registry_.*|verify_system)\\.py$"
        - "(?i)^.*(ID_CANONICALITY|ID_PATTERNS|COUNTER_STORE).*\\.(json|yaml|yml)$"
        - "(?i)^.*(dir_id|file_id|doc_id).*\\.(md|json|yaml|yml|py)$"
      content_regex:
        - "(?i)\\bfile_id\\b"
        - "(?i)\\bdir_id\\b"
        - "(?i)\\bdoc_id\\b"
        - "(?i)\\bCOUNTER_STORE\\b"
        - "(?i)\\bCOUNTER_MAX\\b"
        - "(?i)\\b\\^\\\\d\\{20\\}\\$\\b"   # pattern for 20 digits in regex literals

  # ------------------------------
  # 3) MULTI_CLI_CONVERSION_PLANNING
  # ------------------------------
  - rule_id: "MCP-001"
    priority: 3
    section: "MULTI_CLI_CONVERSION_PLANNING"
    match_any:
      path_globs:
        - "**/LP_LONG_PLAN/**"
        - "**/LONG_PLAN/**"
        - "**/MASTER_SPLINTER/**"
        - "**/newPhasePlanProcess/**"
        - "**/phase*/**"
        - "**/sections/**"
        - "**/plans/**"
      name_regex:
        - "(?i)^\\d{20}_PH-\\d{3}.*\\.(json|md|yaml|yml)$"
        - "(?i)^.*(phase_contract|phase_plan|execution_plan|plan_process|plan_cli|wiring|orchestrator).*\\.(json|md|py|yaml|yml)$"
        - "(?i)^sec_\\d{3}.*\\.(json|md|yaml|yml)$"
      content_regex:
        - "(?i)\"phase\"\\s*:"
        - "(?i)\"tasks\"\\s*:"
        - "(?i)\"artifacts\"\\s*:"
        - "(?i)\"validations\"\\s*:"
        - "(?i)langgraph"
        - "(?i)langchain"
        - "(?i)multi-?cli"
        - "(?i)conversion"
        - "(?i)bridge"
        - "(?i)envelope_contract"

  # ------------------------------
  # 4) TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS
  # ------------------------------
  - rule_id: "TPL-001"
    priority: 4
    section: "TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS"
    match_any:
      path_globs:
        - "**/templates/**"
        - "**/*template*/**"
        - "**/PATCHES/**"
        - "**/*patch*/**"
        - "**/template_ops/**"
      name_regex:
        - "(?i)^.*(TEMPLATE|AUTONOMOUS_DELIVERY_TEMPLATE|TEMPLATE_V\\d+).*\\.(json|yaml|yml|md)$"
        - "(?i)^.*\\.patch\\.json$"
        - "(?i)^.*rfc-?6902.*\\.json$"
        - "(?i)^.*(apply_patch|patch_apply|template_expand|template_render|template_validate).*\\.(py|ps1)$"
      content_regex:
        - "(?i)RFC-?6902"
        - "(?i)\"op\"\\s*:\\s*\"(add|remove|replace|move|copy|test)\""
        - "(?i)\"path\"\\s*:\\s*\"/"

# Tie-break policy (only used if you choose to allow tie-breaks; default is fail-closed).
tie_break:
  enabled: false
  strategy: "FAIL_CLOSED"



Concrete rule set (JSON alternative)


If you need strict JSON (easier for a CLI), say so and I’ll output the exact same rules as JSON (no comments, no trailing commas).



Non-negotiable “ambiguous” cases (explicit)


Mark as AMBIGUOUS (block auto-patch) if any apply:


Path matches **/REGISTRY/** but filename also matches ID_CANONICALITY|COUNTER_STORE|ID_PATTERNS.

A file matches both MCP-001 and TPL-001 by path and name (example: template stored under planning folder).

A file matches ID-001 only by content regex (weak signal) and also matches any other section by path/name.



What you do with these rules


Your classifier should produce, per file:


file_id, relative_path

match_rule_id, section

match_basis = path|name|content|metadata

is_ambiguous boolean + ambiguous_rules[]

target_module_dir_id (from section → dir_id mapping)


Then you can patch /files/<index>/module_id for non-ambiguous matches only.

Systematic process for moving files + updating everything that must change


Core principle


Treat the move as a transaction with explicit inputs and outputs:


Inputs: a deterministic move map (old → new), plus rules for what references are allowed.

Outputs: moved files + updated imports/refs + updated registries + passing gates + evidence bundle.


Do not “move then fix ad-hoc.” Build the map and the patch set first.



Phase 0 — Preconditions (must be true before any move)


Registry module_id already correct (your current task).

Section folders exist and have dir_ids (the target module folders).

Repo in clean state (no uncommitted changes).

You can run a full test/gate pass before and after.


Artifacts to produce:


move_refactor/run_<run_id>/pre_state/
git_status.txt

registry_hashes_before.json

test_summary_before.json

gate_summary_before.json



Phase 1 — Build the deterministic MOVE MAP (plan the refactor)


Create a single SSOT file that defines every move:


MOVE_MAP.json


moves[]: { file_id, old_relative_path, new_relative_path, reason_section, target_module_dir_id }

constraints: { no_overwrites, preserve_case, preserve_extension, allow_collisions:false }

scope: { include_sections:[...], exclude_globs:[...] }


Rules for new path should be purely derivable:


new_relative_path = <section_root>/<subgroup>/<original_filename>

subgroup can be based on artifact_kind, layer, or filetype (but must be deterministic).


Also produce:


collision_report.json (files that would conflict in same target folder)

unresolved_report.json (files you can’t place deterministically)


Fail-closed: any collision/unresolved blocks the move.



Phase 2 — Inventory all reference types that will break


Before moving anything, build a reference index.


Generate:
REFERENCE_INDEX.json


python_imports[] (from AST parse, not regex)

powershell_refs[] (dot-sourcing, relative script paths)

mql4_includes[] (if applicable)

json_yaml_path_refs[] (config keys that contain paths)

docs_refs[] (Markdown links, file mentions)

registry_refs[] (any registry fields that store file paths besides the main registry)


For each record include:


ref_type

source_file

source_file_id (if available)

matched_text

resolved_target (if resolvable)

confidence

will_break_if_moved boolean


This is where you detect “other updates that are needed” besides imports:


CLI config files pointing to old paths

hardcoded paths in scripts

docs and diagrams that link to file paths

wiring specs listing script locations


Fail-closed rules:


If a reference cannot be mapped to a moved target deterministically, it is BLOCKING.



Phase 3 — Derive the UPDATE PATCH SET (before the move)


Create a single “refactor patch plan” that you can apply mechanically:


A) Python import refactor plan


Decide one of these patterns (pick one and stick to it):


Option A (best long-term): package-based imports


Ensure each module folder is a Python package (__init__.py).

Convert intra-repo imports to absolute package imports rooted at a stable top-level package (e.g., govreg_core.*).


Option B (minimal disruption): keep execution model, update relative imports


Only update import statements that reference moved modules.

Keep entrypoint scripts intact, avoid changing Python packaging rules.


For Python, generate:


PY_IMPORT_REWRITE.json
changes[]: { source_file, old_import, new_import }


Use AST-based rewriting (not search/replace), otherwise you’ll miss:


from x import y

aliased imports

multiline imports

conditional imports


B) Non-Python rewrites


Generate:


PATH_REWRITE.json
changes[]: { file, old_string, new_string, context }
For:

.ps1 dot-sourcing

.json/.yaml config file paths

.md links


C) Registry path update plan


Even if module_id is already set, the move changes:


relative_path

possibly directory_path

anything derived from path


Generate:


REGISTRY_PATH_PATCH.rfc6902.json (or a deterministic “sync” run output)

plus REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT.json


Fail-closed: do not proceed unless the patch set is complete and non-ambiguous.



Phase 4 — Execute the move (atomic + reversible)


Create a new branch: refactor/move_modules_<run_id>

Execute moves using git mv driven strictly by MOVE_MAP.json

Immediately run a filesystem verification:
every old_relative_path no longer exists

every new_relative_path exists

file counts match

no unintended changes to file contents (hash compare for non-updated files)


Artifacts:


move_execution_log.jsonl

file_hashes_after_move.json



Phase 5 — Apply rewrite patches (imports + paths + wiring)


Apply AST-based Python import rewrites.

Apply deterministic path rewrites for PS/config/docs.

Add/update __init__.py files if using Option A.

Fix entrypoints:
ensure CLIs still point at correct script/module locations

update any “tool registry” files that list script paths (if you have them)


Artifacts:


rewrite_apply_log.jsonl

diff_summary.md (machine-generated, not narrative)



Phase 6 — Update registries and derived artifacts


Run your registry sync tooling to reconcile new paths:


update registry records for moved files

regenerate derived indexes/manifests if you have them

verify the registry remains schema-valid and internally consistent


Hard invariants:


no file_id changes

no duplicate file_id

registry file counts preserved

every moved file’s registry relative_path matches disk


Artifacts:


registry_hashes_after.json

registry_validation_report.json

path_mismatch_report.json (must be empty)



Phase 7 — Verify (tests + gates + runtime smoke)


Run in this order:


Schema validation (registry + key specs)

Import validation
Python: python -m compileall and/or import smoke test

Unit/integration tests

Gate suite

CLI smoke
run each CLI entrypoint with --help

run a minimal “no-op” run that exercises message passing


Artifacts:


test_summary_after.json

gate_summary_after.json

cli_smoke_log.jsonl


Fail-closed: no merges unless all pass.



Phase 8 — Stabilize against drift (so it doesn’t regress)


Add or update gates that enforce:


No references to old paths remain (scan against MOVE_MAP.json old paths).

Imports remain canonical (no new “relative hacks”).

Registry paths match disk.

Section ownership (module_id) remains consistent with classification rules.



Minimal “checklist” of updates that typically get missed


Python:
__init__.py presence

sys.path hacks in entrypoints (should be removed or updated)

circular imports introduced by reorganizing

PowerShell:
dot-sourcing .\relative\path.ps1

hardcoded absolute paths

JSON/YAML:
tool wiring files pointing at scripts

template pointers (template paths)

Docs:
Markdown links

“how to run” instructions referencing old paths

Registry:
derived manifests and path projections not regenerated

any secondary registries that store paths



If you want this to be fully machine-executable by your CLI, the only extra thing needed is to formalize the four SSOT artifacts that drive everything:


MOVE_MAP.json

REFERENCE_INDEX.json

REWRITE_PATCHSET/ (import/path rewrite plans)

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/ (before/after hashes + reports)


Everything else becomes deterministic execution.

I’ve formalized the four SSOT artifacts and created a complete specification document along with an empty, machine‑readable skeleton you can plug into your CLI workflows.


What’s included


Spec document: Detailed Markdown file (formalized_ssot_spec.md) that defines the structure, required fields and example contents for MOVE_MAP.json, REFERENCE_INDEX.json, REWRITE_PATCHSET/ (split into import and path rewrite plans) and the EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/ directory. It references the JSON Patch specification—operations must specify a verb like add, remove, replace, move, copy or test and include exactly one path fielddatatracker.ietf.org—and highlights why absolute imports are recommended when rewriting Python modulespeps.python.org.

Skeleton JSON/dir tree: A zipped folder (ssot_skeleton.zip) containing blank but schema‑compliant MOVE_MAP.json, REFERENCE_INDEX.json, REWRITE_PATCHSET/ (with empty python_import_rewrites.json and path_rewrites.json) and an EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/ directory. These files are ready to be filled programmatically.


Files to download


Formal SSOT specification (Markdown):
Formalizing the SSOT artifacts for machine‑executability


This document describes four single‑source‑of‑truth (SSOT) artifacts that drive the module refactoring workflow. They are designed to be consumed by a CLI or automation engine. Each artifact has a deterministic structure and a machine‑readable schema so that tools can validate and apply changes consistently. References to JSON Patch operations are drawn from the IETF JSON Patch specification (RFC 6902), which defines that an operation must specify one of several verbs (add, remove, replace, move, copy or test) and include a path member indicating the JSON Pointer locationdatatracker.ietf.org. The rationale for using absolute imports in Python (rather than relative imports) comes from PEP 328, which notes that absolute imports resolve ambiguity and make imports easier to readpeps.python.org.
1 MOVE_MAP.json

Purpose: To define the planned relocation of files from their current locations to new module‑specific folders. This file acts as the single source of truth for the move operation so that it can be audited and replayed.
Top‑level keys:Key
Type
Description
version
string
Schema version of the move map (e.g., "1.0.0").
generated_at
string
ISO 8601 timestamp (UTC) for when the move map was created.
constraints
object
Policy that must hold for the move (no overwrites, extension preservation, etc.).
scope
object
Defines which files are in scope for moving (e.g., includes specific sections and exclude globs).
moves
array
List of move records, each describing a single file relocation.
constraints fields:

no_overwrites (boolean): If true, moving a file into a location that already exists is disallowed.

preserve_extension (boolean): Ensures that the file extension remains unchanged when moving.

allow_collisions (boolean): Defaults to false; if false, collisions block the move.
scope fields:

include_sections (array of strings): Sections whose files can be moved (e.g., "REGISTRY_FILES").

exclude_globs (array of strings): Glob patterns for paths that must never be moved.
Move record object:Key
Type
Description
file_id
string
Unique identifier for the file being moved (from the registry).
old_relative_path
string
Current relative path to the file from the repo root.
new_relative_path
string
Target relative path after the move. This should be derived deterministically from the section folder and file attributes.
reason_section
string
Classification section (e.g., "ID_FILES") that justifies the move.
target_module_dir_id
string
The dir_id of the section folder to which the file will belong.
match_rule_id
string
Identifier of the classification rule that placed the file into a section.
classification_confidence
number
Optional numeric score between 0 and 1 indicating classifier confidence; 1.0 if deterministic.
Example:


{
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "generated_at": "2026-02-15T13:00:00Z",
  "constraints": {
    "no_overwrites": true,
    "preserve_extension": true,
    "allow_collisions": false
  },
  "scope": {
    "include_sections": ["ID_FILES", "REGISTRY_FILES"],
    "exclude_globs": ["**/venv/**", "**/__pycache__/**"]
  },
  "moves": [
    {
      "file_id": "P_20250606123456000000",
      "old_relative_path": "govreg_core/scripts/allocate_ids.py",
      "new_relative_path": "ID_FILES/scripts/allocate_ids.py",
      "reason_section": "ID_FILES",
      "target_module_dir_id": "DIR_12345678901234567890",
      "match_rule_id": "ID-001",
      "classification_confidence": 1.0
    }
  ]
}



2 REFERENCE_INDEX.json


Purpose: To capture every reference in the repository that might break when files are moved. This file is used to generate the rewrite patch set and to ensure that there are no unresolved references.
Top‑level keys:

Key
Type
Description
version
string
Schema version of the reference index.
generated_at
string
ISO 8601 timestamp for when the index was produced.
references
array
A list of reference entries.
unresolved
array
Optional list of references that the tool could not resolve to a target file.
Reference entry fields:
Field
Type
Description
---
---
---
ref_type
string
Kind of reference (e.g., "python_import", "powershell_ref", "json_path", "markdown_link", "registry_ref").
source_file
string
Relative path of the file that contains the reference.
source_file_id
string
The file’s file_id from the registry (optional but recommended).
line_number
integer
The line number in source_file where the reference occurs (for context).
matched_text
string
Literal text of the reference as seen in the source file.
resolved_target
string
Relative path of the file or directory that the reference points to, if the tool can resolve it.
target_file_id
string
The file_id of the referenced file, if known.
will_break_if_moved
boolean
Whether this reference will be broken by moving the target file (true if the reference uses a relative path or package import that changes).
confidence
number
Optional confidence score in the resolver’s match (0–1).
Example:

{
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "generated_at": "2026-02-15T13:05:00Z",
  "references": [
    {
      "ref_type": "python_import",
      "source_file": "govreg_core/main.py",
      "source_file_id": "P_20250512123456000001",
      "line_number": 10,
      "matched_text": "from scripts.allocate_ids import allocate_file_id",
      "resolved_target": "govreg_core/scripts/allocate_ids.py",
      "target_file_id": "P_20250606123456000000",
      "will_break_if_moved": true,
      "confidence": 1.0
    },
    {
      "ref_type": "markdown_link",
      "source_file": "docs/architecture.md",
      "source_file_id": "P_20250401123456000002",
      "line_number": 45,
      "matched_text": "[link](../govreg_core/scripts/allocate_ids.py)",
      "resolved_target": "govreg_core/scripts/allocate_ids.py",
      "target_file_id": "P_20250606123456000000",
      "will_break_if_moved": true,
      "confidence": 1.0
    }
  ],
  "unresolved": []
}


Usage: A CLI should parse this file to determine which references require rewrites. Any entry with will_break_if_moved set to true must appear in the rewrite patch set.



3 REWRITE_PATCHSET/


Purpose: To house deterministic rewrite instructions for updating references after files are moved. The patch set is split by language or file type to allow specialized tooling. Each rewrite file contains an ordered list of changes to apply.


3.1 python_import_rewrites.json


Used for updating Python import statements to maintain correctness. PEP 328 recommends that absolute imports be used by default to avoid ambiguitypeps.python.org; the rewrite plan should therefore convert any relative or outdated imports to absolute ones when files move.
Top‑level keys:

Key
Type
Description
version
string
Schema version of the patch set.
generated_at
string
ISO 8601 timestamp for when the patch file was created.
changes
array
List of import rewrite operations.
Each change object in changes contains:
Field
Type
Description
---
---
---
source_file
string
Relative path of the file where the import occurs.
source_file_id
string
File identifier.
old_import
string
The full import statement as found in the source code.
new_import
string
The new import statement after rewriting (usually absolute).
line_number
integer
Line number where the import occurs.
context
string
Optional snippet of code around the import for human auditing.
Example:

{
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "generated_at": "2026-02-15T13:10:00Z",
  "changes": [
    {
      "source_file": "govreg_core/main.py",
      "source_file_id": "P_20250512123456000001",
      "line_number": 10,
      "old_import": "from scripts.allocate_ids import allocate_file_id",
      "new_import": "from ID_FILES.scripts.allocate_ids import allocate_file_id",
      "context": "# rewrite because allocate_ids moved into ID_FILES"
    }
  ]
}


3.2 path_rewrites.json


Captures rewrites for non‑Python references such as PowerShell dot‑sourcing commands, JSON/YAML configuration paths and Markdown links.
Top‑level keys: Same as for Python import rewrites (version, generated_at, changes). Each change object contains:

Field
Type
Description
source_file
string
Relative path of the file containing the reference.
source_file_id
string
Identifier of that file.
line_number
integer
Line number where the rewrite should occur.
old_string
string
The literal text that needs replacing.
new_string
string
The replacement text.
context
string
Optional context for auditing.
Example:

{
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "generated_at": "2026-02-15T13:10:00Z",
  "changes": [
    {
      "source_file": "docs/architecture.md",
      "source_file_id": "P_20250401123456000002",
      "line_number": 45,
      "old_string": "../govreg_core/scripts/allocate_ids.py",
      "new_string": "../ID_FILES/scripts/allocate_ids.py",
      "context": "Markdown link update after move"
    }
  ]
}


Directory structure


REWRITE_PATCHSET/ may contain additional files grouped by language (e.g., powershell_rewrites.json) if needed. Each file should adhere to the same top‑level structure (version, generated_at, changes).
JSON Patch compatibility: Some tools may prefer to express rewrites as a JSON Patch document. In that case, each rewrite could be translated to a JSON Patch operation; for example, replacing a string in a JSON document uses the replace operation with a path pointing to the field and the value set to the new textdatatracker.ietf.org.



4 EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/


Purpose: To preserve a tamper‑evident trail of the refactoring. The bundle contains both pre‑ and post‑move evidence so that the process can be audited and repeated. Evidence files should be immutable once produced.
Recommended content:


before/ and after/ hashes – JSON files containing SHA‑256 or similar hashes of every file in the repository before and after the move. These hashes prove that file contents were not altered unexpectedly. Example keys:
file_id

relative_path

sha256

classification_rules.json – A copy of the deterministic classification rules that assigned each file to a section. Keeping the rules here ensures reproducibility.

classification_report.json – The report that lists every file along with its classification outcome (section, match_rule_id, is_ambiguous, etc.).

move_map.json – The finalized move plan (duplicate of MOVE_MAP.json for provenance). This file ensures that the evidence bundle is self‑contained.

reference_index.json – The reference index used to generate the rewrite patch set. Retaining this document in the evidence bundle helps auditors trace each rewrite back to its source.

rewrite_patchset/ – Copies of the files from the REWRITE_PATCHSET/ directory so that reviewers can see the exact rewrite instructions applied.

registry_patch.json – The JSON Patch document that updates the registry file after the move. The patch should use the operations defined in RFC 6902 (e.g., replace with a path and valuedatatracker.ietf.org). For example, replacing a module identifier uses the replace operation with a path like /files/42/module_id and value equal to the new dir_id.

apply_log.jsonl – A newline‑delimited JSON (JSONL) log capturing the exact steps performed when executing the refactor (ordered timestamps, actions, success/failure statuses). Each entry may contain:
timestamp (ISO 8601)

action (e.g., move_file, apply_patch)

entity (path or file identifier)

status (success or error)

details (error message or additional context)
Directory structure example:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/
├── before_hashes.json
├── after_hashes.json
├── classification_rules.json
├── classification_report.json
├── move_map.json
├── reference_index.json
├── rewrite_patchset/
│   ├── python_import_rewrites.json
│   └── path_rewrites.json
├── registry_patch.json
└── apply_log.jsonl


Rationale: Creating a self‑contained evidence bundle allows anyone to verify that the refactor adhered to the declared policies and that no unexpected file modifications occurred. The JSON Patch specification requires that operation objects include a single path member referring to the target locationdatatracker.ietf.org, and only allowed operations (add, remove, replace, move, copy, test) appear in the patchdatatracker.ietf.org. Including the patch in the evidence bundle ensures that auditors can replay the changes.



Summary


By formalizing the SSOT artifacts as described above, you ensure that the module refactoring process is transparent, deterministic and fully machine‑executable. The MOVE_MAP defines what is being moved and why; the REFERENCE_INDEX enumerates every reference that might break; the REWRITE_PATCHSET prescribes precise textual rewrites (favoring absolute importspeps.python.org); and the EVIDENCE_BUNDLE preserves all inputs, outputs and proofs of execution. Together, these artifacts form a robust foundation for automated CLI tools to perform large‑scale codebase reorganizations safely and reproducibly.

Skeleton directory archive (ZIP): ssot_skeleton.zip


The spec covers field names, data types, example objects and rationale for each artifact, ensuring the entire refactor process is deterministic and auditable. The skeleton provides a starting point for your tooling to populate with real data.

Thought for 18s


AI Execution Instructions: SSOT-Driven Refactor (Registry → IDs/Modules → Planning → Templates)


Authoritative inputs (do not invent)


Use these as ground truth for structure, classification signals, and current registry shape:


Final consolidated status + readiness context:
01260207201000000133_FINAL_CONS…

File classification signals + decision tree:
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE

Unified registry (structure + column headers + file list):
01999000042260124503_governance…

Registry path update precedent (fields updated + backup behavior):
REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT

Existing registry sync utility behavior (patterns for layer/artifact_kind derivation):
P_01260207201000000197_sync_reg…

Recursive file-id assignment utility (naming + exclusions + allocator usage):
P_01260207201000000198_add_ids_…

Counter store format (do not mutate outside controlled runs):
01260207201000000116_COUNTER_ST…



Objective (what “done” means)


You will implement a deterministic, CLI-executable refactor driven ONLY by four SSOT artifacts:


MOVE_MAP.json

REFERENCE_INDEX.json

REWRITE_PATCHSET/ (rewrite plans)

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/ (proof: hashes + reports)


Acceptance outcome:


Registry updated first (module assignment points to destination dir_id).

Files moved second (filesystem matches MOVE_MAP).

Imports/paths rewritten third (codebase compiles/tests pass).

Evidence bundle proves: before/after hashes, registry diffs, rewrite diffs, move logs, validation results.



Hard rules (lockdown)


1.1 No implied behavior


If a transformation is not expressed in SSOT artifacts, do not perform it.


1.2 Two-phase apply


Phase A: “Plan + Patch” (generate SSOT, patch registry, validate; no file moves)

Phase B: “Move + Rewrite” (perform moves, apply rewrites, validate; no registry guessing)


1.3 Deterministic ordering


All lists MUST be processed in a stable order:


by file_id ascending, then

by source_relpath ascending.


1.4 Fail-closed + rollback-ready


If any validation fails, stop immediately and write:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/rollback_instructions.md

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/failure_fingerprint.json


1.5 Registry update policy


Follow the established pattern: update only specified fields, create backups, produce update counts (as in prior registry path update run).


REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT



Define the 4 SSOT artifacts (formal)


2.1 MOVE_MAP.json (single source of truth for relocation)


Purpose: declares the only allowed file relocations.


Minimum fields per record:


file_id (20-digit)

source_relpath

dest_dir_id (20-digit directory ID)

dest_dir_relpath (dir folder name including dir_id prefix)

dest_relpath (dest_dir_relpath + filename)

category (REGISTRY_FILES | ID_FILES | MULTI_CLI_PLANNING | TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS)

confidence (HIGH|MED|LOW)

match_signals[] (why it matched; deterministic strings)

move_enabled (bool; default false until validated)


Global fields:


schema_version

generated_utc

repo_root

dir_id_resolution block (how dest_dir_id was discovered)

counts_by_category


2.2 REFERENCE_INDEX.json (single source of truth for what must be rewritten)


Purpose: enumerates every reference that must change as a result of the move.


Minimum fields per reference record:


ref_id (stable hash of: file_id + old_text + new_text + location)

source_file_id

source_relpath

ref_type (PY_IMPORT | FROM_IMPORT | PATH_STRING | JSON_POINTER | YAML_PATH | MD_LINK | CLI_ARG | POWERSHELL_PATH | OTHER)

old_value

new_value

location (line/col or JSON pointer if structured)

rewrite_plan_id (links into REWRITE_PATCHSET)

confidence

evidence (snippet hash, not raw content)


2.3 REWRITE_PATCHSET/ (machine-executable rewrite plans)


Purpose: expresses how changes are applied, without performing them yet.


Directory layout:


REWRITE_PATCHSET/manifest.json

REWRITE_PATCHSET/plans/<rewrite_plan_id>.json


Each plan must include:


rewrite_plan_id

target_file_id

target_relpath

strategy:
TEXT_REPLACE_SET (ordered replacements)

PY_IMPORT_REWRITE (AST-based if available; else deterministic regex set)

JSON_POINTER_PATCH (RFC-6902 ops)

pre_hash_sha256

post_hash_sha256_expected (optional if computed post-apply)

operations[] (explicit; no wildcards unless bounded + justified)

dry_run_output_path (where the diff preview will be written)


2.4 EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/ (proof of correctness)


Purpose: stores before/after proofs and run reports.


Required outputs:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/run_manifest.json (run_id, tool versions, timestamps)

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/before_snapshot/:
registry_hashes.json

file_hashes.json (sha256 for all moved + rewritten targets)

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/after_snapshot/ (same structure)

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/:
move_plan_report.md

registry_patch_report.md

rewrite_plan_report.md

rewrite_apply_report.md

validation_report.md

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/diffs/:
registry.patch.rfc6902.json

rewrite_diffs/ (unified diffs per file)

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/logs/:
move.log

rewrite.log

errors.jsonl



Concrete classification rules for the 4 target categories


Use directory signals + filename patterns + content heuristics. Prefer “directory signals” first (as in quick reference).


CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE


3.1 Category: REGISTRY_FILES


A file is REGISTRY_FILES if ANY are true:


Path contains a registry root folder (example: .../REGISTRY...) or filename contains registry AND artifact is structured config (.json/.yaml).

The file is the unified registry itself or any derived registry projections.

Strong indicator: registry schema/headers like column_headers, files[], entries[] (see unified registry structure).
01999000042260124503_governance…


3.2 Category: ID_FILES


A file is ID_FILES if ANY are true:


Name contains: ID_CANONICALITY, COUNTER_STORE, id_allocator, canonical_id_patterns, DIR_ID, FILE_ID.

It is part of ID allocation / ID validation / ID gates (as described in the ID system execution report patterns and scripts).
01260207201000000134_FINAL_EXEC…
P_01260207201000000198_add_ids_…
01260207201000000116_COUNTER_ST…


3.3 Category: MULTI_CLI_PLANNING


A file is MULTI_CLI_PLANNING if ANY are true:


Located under planning areas (LP_LONG_PLAN, PHASE_* planning folders, “phase contract”, “planning”, “execution plan”).
CLASSIFICATION_QUICK_REFERENCE

Contains terms indicating tool-to-tool orchestration: workstreams, gates, phase_contract, envelope_contract, mutation set, plan conversion, multi-cli, orchestrator.


3.4 Category: TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS


A file is TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS if ANY are true:


It is a template definition or template executor (filenames contain TEMPLATE, template, AUTONOMOUS_DELIVERY_TEMPLATE, “template operations”).

It performs structured patching / generation over canonical artifacts (e.g., apply patch atomic / patch plans).


3.5 Ambiguity rule (must be explicit)


If multiple categories match, pick ONE by precedence:


REGISTRY_FILES

ID_FILES

TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS

MULTI_CLI_PLANNING
Record all matches in match_signals[], but only one category.



Directory targets (module folders) and dir_id binding


4.1 You must resolve destination folders to real dir_ids


Do not fabricate dest_dir_id. Resolve by reading:


existing directory naming convention with dir_id prefixes (as used in path update run).
REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT

directory .dir_id metadata where present (DIR-ID governance system is production-ready).
01260207201000000133_FINAL_CONS…


4.2 “module_id in registry” update


Wherever the unified registry stores a module assignment (e.g., module_id, or equivalent field), you will set it to the destination folder’s dir_id. If the registry does not yet have module_id, you will:


add a new column/header in column_headers for module_dir_id (or your chosen canonical name), and

populate it for every file in MOVE_MAP.json.


All changes must be represented as an RFC-6902 patch and stored in evidence. (Pattern consistent with prior patch tracking in evidence).



CLI-executable end-to-end steps


Step 1 — Create a branch + run_id


Create a new git branch for this refactor.

Generate run_id (timestamp + random suffix) and store it in EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/run_manifest.json.


Step 2 — Build candidate inventory (no changes)


From repo root:


Load unified registry file and build a lookup: file_id -> current relative_path from files[].
01999000042260124503_governance…

Scan filesystem to confirm paths exist (report missing/stale).


Output:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/inventory_consistency_report.md

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/before_snapshot/file_hashes.json (only for candidate files)


Step 3 — Classify files into the 4 categories


Apply the concrete rules in §3 to every file in registry and/or filesystem.

Produce deterministic match signals.


Output:


MOVE_MAP.json with move_enabled=false for all records initially

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/classification_report.md


Step 4 — Resolve destination dirs + produce final MOVE_MAP


For each category, map to a destination “section folder” (one per category). For each folder:


resolve its dir_id

write dest_dir_id and dest_dir_relpath


Then compute each dest_relpath deterministically.


Flip move_enabled=true only if:


destination dir exists and has valid dir_id

source file exists

no dest path collision


Output:


updated MOVE_MAP.json

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/move_plan_report.md


Step 5 — Generate REFERENCE_INDEX (dry scan)


Scan all files that may reference moved files:


Python imports (import x, from x import y)

path strings referencing old relpaths

JSON/YAML references (known pointers, config fields)

markdown links


For each hit, compute deterministic new_value based on MOVE_MAP.


Output:


REFERENCE_INDEX.json


Step 6 — Generate REWRITE_PATCHSET (dry-run)


For each impacted file in REFERENCE_INDEX.json:


create a rewrite plan (REWRITE_PATCHSET/plans/<id>.json)

compute pre_hash_sha256

write dry-run diffs (do not modify files yet)


Output:


REWRITE_PATCHSET/manifest.json

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/rewrite_plan_report.md


Step 7 — Patch registry FIRST (no file moves)


Create RFC-6902 patch that:


updates module_dir_id (or your canonical module field) for all moved files

optionally updates relative_path only if your registry policy requires it pre-move (recommended: keep current relpath until after move, then do a second patch; be explicit which policy you choose)


Apply patch, write backup, and write update counts (same style as registry path update report).


Output:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/diffs/registry.patch.rfc6902.json

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/registry_patch_report.md


Step 8 — Validate registry integrity (gates)


Run:


schema validation (registry schema rules)

uniqueness checks (file_id uniqueness, no duplicate paths)

module_dir_id populated for moved cohort


Output:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/validation_report.md (Phase A)


Step 9 — Move files (filesystem)


Execute moves strictly from MOVE_MAP.json where move_enabled=true.
Requirements:


log every move

verify dest exists

verify post-move sha256 equals pre-move sha256 (content unchanged)


Output:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/logs/move.log

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/after_snapshot/file_hashes.json (moved files)


Step 10 — Apply rewrites (imports/paths)


Apply REWRITE_PATCHSET plans:


enforce pre_hash_sha256 match (fail if drift)

apply operations

write unified diff per file

compute post-hash


Output:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/diffs/rewrite_diffs/*

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/rewrite_apply_report.md

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/logs/rewrite.log


Step 11 — Patch registry SECOND (final paths)


Now update registry relative_path/directory_path/absolute_path fields for moved files to match filesystem (exactly like the prior directory rename path update behavior).


REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT


Output:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/diffs/registry_paths_after_move.patch.rfc6902.json

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/registry_after_move_report.md


Step 12 — Full validation and tests


run unit/integration tests

run any gates that enforce import hygiene / canonicality


Output:


EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/validation_report.md (Phase B)

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/after_snapshot/registry_hashes.json


Step 13 — Commit set


Commit, in order:


SSOT artifacts (MOVE_MAP/REFERENCE_INDEX/REWRITE_PATCHSET)

registry patch(es)

moves + rewrites

evidence bundle (or store evidence outside git if policy says so; be explicit)



Required CLI commands (express as a CLI plan; do not handwave)


You will implement or invoke CLI commands that map 1:1 to the steps above, e.g.:


cli refactor plan --out MOVE_MAP.json --evidence EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/

cli refactor references --move-map MOVE_MAP.json --out REFERENCE_INDEX.json

cli refactor rewrite-plan --reference-index REFERENCE_INDEX.json --out REWRITE_PATCHSET/

cli registry patch --registry <path> --patch <rfc6902.json> --backup

cli refactor move --move-map MOVE_MAP.json

cli refactor rewrite-apply --patchset REWRITE_PATCHSET/

cli validate all --evidence EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/


If these CLIs don’t exist yet, you must create minimal scripts that implement them with deterministic args and outputs; do not embed logic in ad-hoc manual commands.



Output checklist (must exist at end)


MOVE_MAP.json

REFERENCE_INDEX.json

REWRITE_PATCHSET/manifest.json + per-file plans

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/ with:
before/after hashes

registry patches (RFC-6902)

move + rewrite logs

validation reports



Non-negotiable behaviors


Never move a file not present in MOVE_MAP.json.

Never rewrite a file not present in REWRITE_PATCHSET/.

Never update registry fields not declared in the registry patch.

Always produce backups before registry writes (consistent with prior runs).
REGISTRY_PATH_UPDATE_REPORT

Always preserve file content during moves (hash equality check).



If you want this instruction set embedded into your repo as an executable “refactor contract”, store it as a new governed documentation artifact and register it in the unified registry (layer=GOVERNANCE or DOCUMENTATION per your policy).


01999000042260124503_governance…

Updated AI Execution Instructions (apply these overrides to the prior instruction set)


A) Hard constraints (new, non-negotiable)


Only move files and folders that have IDs
A file is eligible to move only if it has a valid file_id (20-digit) in the unified registry AND/OR the filename is ID-prefixed (e.g., P_<20digits>_*).


A folder is eligible to move only if it has a valid dir_id (20-digit) assigned and the folder name is ID-prefixed (e.g., <20digits>_NAME).

Any file/folder lacking an ID is ineligible and must be reported as SKIPPED_NO_ID.
Human review required before any move


Before executing git mv / filesystem moves, AI must generate a single review document listing every planned move with the current complete file path (absolute path) for each file that will move.

AI must stop after producing this review artifact. No move execution occurs until a human approves.



B) Canonical destination folders (fixed targets)


All moved files MUST land in exactly one of these four destination roots (no new roots):


REGISTRY_FILES →
C:\Users\richg\Gov_Reg\01260207201000001250_REGISTRY

ID_FILES →
C:\Users\richg\Gov_Reg\01260207201000001162_GEU\01260207201000001165_ID

MULTI_CLI_PLANNING →
C:\Users\richg\Gov_Reg\01260207201000001221_Multi CLI

TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS →
C:\Users\richg\Gov_Reg\newPhasePlanProcess


Rules:


Do not change these paths.

Do not derive alternative destinations.

The dest_dir_id must be taken from the folder prefix in each destination path.



C) Required new artifact: Human Move Review Document


File name + location (deterministic)


Write to:
EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/HUMAN_MOVE_REVIEW.md


Required contents (must include)


For each move record, include:


file_id

category (one of the four)

current_absolute_path (FULL path)

current_relative_path (from C:\Users\richg\Gov_Reg\)

destination_root_absolute_path (one of the four roots above)

destination_absolute_path (FULL path after move)

destination_relative_path

source_dir_id (if known)

dest_dir_id (must match destination root folder prefix)

move_enabled (true only if all eligibility checks pass)


Also include summary totals:


total candidates

total eligible

skipped (no file_id)

skipped (ambiguous category)

skipped (destination collision)


Stop condition


After writing HUMAN_MOVE_REVIEW.md, AI must:


set MOVE_MAP.json records to move_enabled=false by default

optionally produce a second file MOVE_MAP_APPROVAL.json placeholder

terminate execution (do not perform moves)



D) Updated MOVE_MAP requirements (destination mapping locked)


For each category, destination roots are fixed:


REGISTRY_FILES → 01260207201000001250_REGISTRY

ID_FILES → 01260207201000001165_ID

MULTI_CLI_PLANNING → 01260207201000001221_Multi CLI

TEMPLATE_OPERATIONS → newPhasePlanProcess (this one is not ID-prefixed; therefore only move items inside it that already have file_id/dir_id)


Special rule: newPhasePlanProcess root is not ID-prefixed


You may move files with file_id into that folder.

You may move folders with dir_id into that folder.

You must not move any non-ID items there “just because they belong.”



E) Updated execution sequence (inserted human gate)


Phase A — Plan + Patch (no moves)


Build classification + candidate list (unchanged)

Apply “only items with ID” eligibility filter (new)

Build MOVE_MAP.json with the locked destination roots (new)

Generate REFERENCE_INDEX.json and REWRITE_PATCHSET/ (unchanged)

Patch registry module fields (unchanged)


Phase A.5 — Human Review Gate (new)


Generate EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/HUMAN_MOVE_REVIEW.md (new)

STOP (no moves)


Phase B — Move + Rewrite (only after human approval)


After approval, set move_enabled=true for approved items only

Move files/folders (ID-only) strictly from MOVE_MAP

Apply rewrites from REWRITE_PATCHSET

Patch registry paths (post-move)

Validate, test, evidence



F) Additional validations (must run before HUMAN_MOVE_REVIEW is produced)


Existence check
Confirm each current_absolute_path exists.


Collision check


Confirm each destination_absolute_path does not exist (unless explicitly permitted; default is fail).


Registry alignment check


Confirm registry relative_path for file_id matches the filesystem (or emit as MISMATCH_REGISTRY_VS_FS and block move).


ID integrity check


file_id is exactly 20 digits.


dir_id is exactly 20 digits.



G) Update to “only move folders that have id”


When MOVE_MAP includes directories:


Add entity_type: "file"|"dir"

For dir entities, require:
folder name begins with the 20-digit dir_id

registry (or dir-id registry) confirms assignment

If either fails: SKIPPED_NO_DIR_ID



H) Required outputs (end of Phase A)


Must exist before stopping:


MOVE_MAP.json (with move_enabled=false by default)

REFERENCE_INDEX.json

REWRITE_PATCHSET/

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/HUMAN_MOVE_REVIEW.md

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/move_plan_report.md

EVIDENCE_BUNDLE/reports/registry_patch_report.md

Registry patch artifacts (RFC-6902)


No filesystem moves should have occurred yet.
